title: Be Proactive: Own Your Choices
rawContent: Being proactive is about taking charge of your life, much like an artist who controls their brush to create a masterpiece. This means owning your choices and being responsible for the outcomes, whether they are positive or negative. By doing so, you become the captain of your ship, steering it in the direction you desire. Proactivity involves not only anticipating challenges but also setting goals and taking deliberate steps to overcome obstacles. It's about actively seeking solutions and opportunities for growth, rather than being a passive bystander. By seizing the pen to write your own story, you can pursue your dreams and aspirations with purpose, resilience, and determination.

script.mixed:
[HOOK]
Today I want to show you what it actually means to be proactive—and it's not what most people think.

[BODY]
See, being proactive isn't just about planning ahead. It's about owning your choices. Let me give you an example. When something goes wrong at work, a reactive person says: "My boss didn't give me clear instructions." A proactive person says: "I should have asked better questions." The difference? One blames the situation. The other takes responsibility. Now, here's the thing: when you own your choices, you also own the power to change them. If you failed because you didn't prepare, you can prepare next time. If you got rejected because you didn't practice, you can practice more. The moment you stop blaming outside forces, you become the person steering the ship.

[CLOSE]
Next time something goes wrong, ask yourself: what could I have done differently? That's how you take back control.

title: Proactivity as a Blank Canvas
rawContent: Imagine your life as a blank canvas where you have the power to paint your own masterpiece. Being proactive means taking charge of your actions and decisions to shape your destiny, much like an artist controls their brush to create art. This concept emphasizes that you have the ability to steer your life in the direction you desire by making intentional choices and taking responsibility for the outcomes, whether positive or negative.

script.mixed:
[HOOK]
Today I'm going to show you exactly what it means to be proactive—and why most people get it completely wrong.

[BODY]
See, being proactive isn't about being busy or saying yes to everything. It's about taking responsibility for your choices and their outcomes. Let me give you an example. When something goes wrong at work, most people blame their boss, the system, the economy. A proactive person asks: what could I have done differently? What can I control next time? The difference is simple: reactive people hand over their power. Proactive people keep it. Now, this doesn't mean you control everything. You don't. But you always control how you respond. And that response? That's what shapes your life.

[CLOSE]
Next time something goes wrong, ask yourself: what part of this can I control? Start there.
script.default:
[HOOK]  
Imagine your life as a blank canvas, and you're the artist with the brush.

[BODY]  
Being proactive is like deciding what to paint on that canvas. It's about taking charge of your actions and decisions, much like an artist chooses each brushstroke. When you're proactive, you shape your destiny by making intentional choices. This means acknowledging that the outcomes—whether they're beautiful or a bit messy—are your responsibility. It's empowering because you get to steer your life in the direction you truly want, rather than letting circumstances dictate your path.

[CLOSE]  
So ask yourself: what masterpiece do you want to create, and what brushstrokes will you choose today?
script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Today I want to show you what it actually means to be proactive—and why most people get it completely wrong.

[BODY]
See, being proactive isn't about doing more stuff or staying busy. It's about taking responsibility for what happens next. Let me give you an example. Let's say you're stuck in a job you hate. A reactive person complains to their friends, scrolls through job boards, but never applies. A proactive person? They update their resume, reach out to three people in their network, and schedule informational interviews—all in one week. The difference isn't motivation. It's ownership. They decided: this is my problem to solve, not something that happens to me. Now, this doesn't mean everything will work out perfectly. But when you take action, you're steering the outcome instead of waiting for someone else to fix it.

[CLOSE]
Pick one thing in your life you've been waiting on. Then ask yourself: what's one action I can take this week to move it forward?

title: Principle of Ownership
rawContent: Being proactive involves taking ownership of your choices and their outcomes. It's about recognizing that you are the captain of your ship, steering it towards your desired direction. This principle highlights the importance of responsibility and accountability in shaping your life. Instead of being a passive bystander, proactive individuals actively seek solutions and opportunities for growth, anticipating challenges and setting goals to overcome obstacles.

script.mixed:
[HOOK]
Today I want to show you the difference between being proactive and reactive—and why one of them keeps you stuck.

[BODY]
See, reactive people wait for things to happen to them. Their boss criticizes them, they feel terrible all day. Traffic makes them late, they blame the roads. They're basically passengers in their own life. Proactive people are different. They're the captain of the ship. When something goes wrong, they ask: what can I do about this? Let's say your boss criticizes your work. A reactive person sulks. A proactive person thinks: okay, what specifically can I improve? Or let's say you're always running late. A reactive person blames traffic. A proactive person leaves 15 minutes earlier. The difference is simple: proactive people take ownership. They don't wait for permission or perfect conditions. They see a problem and they move.

[CLOSE]
Next time something goes wrong, ask yourself: what's one thing I can actually control here? That's how you start steering your own ship.
script.default:
[HOOK]  
Ever feel like life is just happening to you? Well, here's how you can take the wheel and steer it.

[BODY]  
It's all about being proactive. Think of it like being the captain of your own ship. You're not just floating around, you’re actually steering toward where you want to go. This means taking ownership of your choices and their outcomes. Instead of reacting to what happens, you're seeking solutions and opportunities for growth. You anticipate challenges and set goals to overcome them—like plotting a course to navigate through stormy weather. 

[CLOSE]  
So next time you're faced with a decision, remember: you're the captain. Choose your direction.
script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Today I want to show you the difference between being proactive and reactive—and why one makes you feel powerless while the other puts you in control.

[BODY]
See, reactive people blame their circumstances. Bad boss? That's why I'm unhappy. Traffic? That's why I'm late. They hand over control to everything around them. Proactive people do the opposite. They ask: what can I control here? Bad boss? I can update my resume or have a direct conversation. Traffic? I can leave earlier or find a new route. The difference is ownership. Reactive people wait for things to get better. Proactive people make things better. And here's the thing—you don't control what happens to you, but you always control how you respond.

[CLOSE]
Next time something goes wrong, ask yourself: what's one thing I can actually do about this? That question turns you from passenger to captain.

title: Vision as a Blueprint
rawContent: Envision your life as a construction project where you're the architect designing your dream home. Having a clear vision of your goals is akin to a blueprint guiding the construction process. This analogy emphasizes the importance of defining your long-term objectives and values. By clarifying what success means to you, you can align your actions with that vision, ensuring that your daily efforts contribute towards building the life you desire.

script.mixed:
[HOOK]
Today I'm going to show you exactly how to build a life you actually want.

[BODY]
Think of your life like building a house. You're the architect. Now, most people skip the blueprint and just start hammering. They work hard every day but don't know what they're building. Then five years later they look around and think: wait, this isn't what I wanted. See, you need a blueprint first. That means sitting down and asking: what does success actually mean to me? Not your parents' version. Not what Instagram says. Your version. Maybe it's owning a business. Maybe it's having time for your kids. Maybe it's living in a different city. Write it down. Get specific. Because once you know what you're building, every action either moves you closer to that house or it doesn't.

[CLOSE]
Spend 20 minutes this week writing your blueprint. What does your dream house actually look like?
script.default:
[HOOK]  
Think of your life as a construction project where you're the architect designing your dream home.

[BODY]  
Imagine you have this blueprint—it's your vision of what you want your life to look like. Just like a builder needs a clear plan to construct a house, you need a defined vision to guide your decisions and actions. This means getting specific about what success and happiness mean to you. Maybe it's a job you love, strong relationships, or financial freedom. By having this blueprint, you ensure that every step you take daily is a brick laid towards the life you truly want.

[CLOSE]  
So, take a moment to sketch out your life's blueprint. What does your dream home look like?
script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Think of your life like building a house. You wouldn't start pouring concrete without a blueprint, right? Same thing with your goals.

[BODY]
See, most people just start doing stuff—working harder, saying yes to everything, chasing whatever comes next. But without a clear picture of what you're building, you end up with a bunch of random rooms that don't connect. Now, here's what actually works: sit down and ask yourself what success looks like five years from now. Not your parents' version. Not what looks good on Instagram. Your version. Maybe it's running your own business. Maybe it's living somewhere quiet. Maybe it's having your weekends completely free. Once you've got that picture, you reverse-engineer it. Every decision you make—every job you take, every skill you learn, every person you spend time with—either builds toward that vision or it doesn't.

[CLOSE]
So grab a notebook. Write down what your finished house looks like. Then ask: does what I'm doing today actually build that?

sourceRef: Ch 3 · Habit 2 - Begin with the End in Mind

title: Teleology vs Aetiology: Goals over Causes
rawContent: Adler's approach differs fundamentally from Freud's. While Freud focused on past causes (aetiology) and determinism—the idea that your past determines your present and future—Adler focuses on present goals (teleology). This shift is profound: instead of being trapped by past trauma, you are free to choose your future. For example, in viewing trauma, Freud would say your suffering stems from a past cause (not your fault), while Adler would say no experience is itself a cause of success or failure. We make of our experiences whatever suits our purposes. The meaning we give experiences is self-determining. This means you are not determined by your past; you determine yourself by the meaning you assign to your experiences. This is the foundation of Adlerian psychology: you have agency, and your future is not written by your history.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Freud says your past controls you. Adler says you control your past. That difference changes everything.

[BODY]
See, Freud believed in aetiology—that your trauma determines who you are today. You're suffering because of what happened to you. Not your fault. Adler flipped it. He said: no experience is a cause of anything. You decide what your experiences mean. Let's say two people get rejected. One thinks: "I'm unlovable." The other thinks: "That person wasn't right for me." Same event. Different meaning. Different life. Freud says you're trapped by your history. Adler says you're free to rewrite it. The meaning you give your past—that's what shapes your future.

[CLOSE]
You're not stuck. You just haven't changed the story yet.

title: Life is Not Linear: A Series of Dots
rawContent: Life is not like climbing a mountain with a single path to the top. If it were, most of life would be spent 'en route,' and failing to reach the summit would invalidate your entire journey. Adler rejects this linear view. Instead, life is a series of dots—a dance, not a climb. Each moment is complete in itself; the process is the outcome. You don't need a story or a predetermined path. The future is a blank page. This view liberates you from the tyranny of a 'well-planned life,' which is impossible anyway. When you see life as cause-and-effect (Freud's view), you try to live according to a script and may blame your past or environment when things go wrong. But that is a life-lie. The past looks like a straight line only if you keep choosing not to change. Life is made up of moments. Focus on the here and now, and you'll find you only live in the present—not in a story about the past or future.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
You think life is like climbing a mountain—one path, one summit. But what if you never reach the top? Does that mean your whole climb was pointless?

[BODY]
See, Adler says life isn't a mountain. It's a dance. Mountains have a single route to the peak. Most of your time is spent "on the way," and if you don't make it, the journey feels wasted. But a dance? Every step is complete in itself. There's no destination—just movement. Now, when you see life as cause-and-effect, you're living from a script. You blame your past when things go wrong. "I'm like this because my parents did that." But the past only looks like a straight line if you keep choosing not to change. Life is made up of moments—dots, not a line. The future is a blank page.

[CLOSE]
Stop climbing toward some imaginary peak. Focus on this moment. That's where you actually live.

title: Feelings of Inferiority Can Drive Growth
rawContent: Everyone has feelings of inferiority, and they are not inherently bad. When we're born, we're helpless babies, and we all want to escape that helpless state—this is the pursuit of superiority. The feeling of inferiority is the counterpart of this pursuit. Both can stimulate normal, healthy striving and growth. A healthy feeling of inferiority comes from comparing yourself to your ideal self, not to others. The key is to move forward on your own feet, not with a competitive mindset. However, people who lack the courage to change may develop an inferiority complex—using their feelings as an excuse ('I am uneducated, so I can't succeed'). This can evolve into a superiority complex, where the person acts superior or attaches themselves to powerful people for reflected glory. Adler believed people boast only out of inferiority; truly confident people don't feel the need to boast. The healthy path is the 'courage to be normal'—accepting that being normal isn't bad, because everybody is normal.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Everyone has feelings of inferiority. And that's not a bad thing. See, when you're born, you're a helpless baby. You can't feed yourself, can't walk, can't do anything. So your brain starts this lifelong pursuit: escape that helpless state. Become capable. That drive is what pushes you forward.

[BODY]
Now, a healthy feeling of inferiority comes from comparing yourself to your ideal self—not to others. Like, "I want to be able to run a marathon," not "I need to beat that guy at the gym." The problem starts when you lack the courage to change. That's when inferiority turns into an excuse. You tell yourself: "I'm uneducated, so I can't succeed." Or "I'm not smart enough, so why try?" And here's where it gets weird. Some people flip it into a superiority complex. They start bragging, name-dropping, attaching themselves to powerful people for reflected glory. Adler said people only boast out of inferiority. Truly confident people don't feel the need to prove anything.

[CLOSE]
The healthy path? Accept that being normal isn't bad. Because everybody is normal. That's the courage to be normal.

sourceRef: Feelings of inferiority can cause change

title: Separate Your Tasks from Others' Tasks
rawContent: All interpersonal relationship problems are caused by intruding on other people's tasks or having others intrude on yours. To separate tasks, ask: who will ultimately bear the consequences of this decision? For example, studying is the child's task, not the parent's. If the child doesn't study, the child bears the consequences. A parent who orders the child to study is intruding on the child's task. Instead, the parent should let the child know studying is his task and offer help when needed. This is not non-interference (not caring at all), but non-intrusion—you can lead a horse to water but can't make it drink. Separating tasks is a way of setting boundaries and makes life less difficult. For example, trusting your partner is your task; how they act is theirs. If you try to control their actions, you intrude on their task. When you separate tasks, you hold all the cards in the relationship. You are responsible for your own life, and others are responsible for theirs.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Today I'm going to show you exactly how to stop feeling responsible for other people's problems.

[BODY]
See, the Japanese have this concept called separation of tasks. And it works like this: ask yourself, who ultimately bears the consequences of this decision? Let me give you an example. Your kid doesn't want to study. You nag, you threaten, you hover. But here's the thing—if your kid fails the test, who suffers? The kid does. Not you. So studying is the kid's task, not yours. Now, this doesn't mean you don't care. You can say: hey, studying is your responsibility, but I'm here if you need help. You can lead a horse to water, but you can't make it drink. Another example: trusting your partner is your task. How they act is theirs. If you're constantly trying to control what they do, you're intruding on their task. And when you do that, you lose your power.

[CLOSE]
Next time you feel responsible for someone's choices, ask: whose task is this really?

sourceRef: Separate your tasks to improve your interpersonal relationships

title: Freedom is Being Disliked by Others
rawContent: Seeking recognition or approval from others is a sign you haven't separated your tasks. If you seek approval, you spend your life following others' expectations, not your own. Freedom is being disliked by other people. If someone dislikes you, that's proof you are exercising your freedom and living according to your own principles. It's natural to not want to be disliked, but to avoid that, you would have to constantly gauge others' feelings and try to please everyone—which is impossible and very un-free. You would be lying to yourself and others, and those lies will catch up to you. This also means you shouldn't expect others to satisfy your expectations. If they don't act how you want, don't get angry—what they do is their task, not yours. Ironically, people who seek recognition are very self-centred. They worry about others only because they want others to think well of them—not out of true concern. When you truly have a feeling of contribution, you have no need for recognition from others.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
If you're constantly seeking approval from people, you're actually being selfish.

[BODY]
See, when you worry about what others think, you're not really concerned about them—you're concerned about yourself. You want them to like you. You want them to think well of you. That's not care, that's self-centeredness. Now, the only way to avoid being disliked is to constantly gauge everyone's feelings and try to please them. But that's impossible. You'd have to lie to yourself and others just to keep up. And those lies will catch up to you. Here's the thing: freedom is being disliked by other people. If someone dislikes you, that's proof you're living according to your own principles, not theirs. And when you truly contribute to others without needing their approval, you stop caring about recognition altogether.

[CLOSE]
Next time you catch yourself seeking approval, ask: am I doing this for them, or for me?

sourceRef: Live for yourself, not for others

title: Build Horizontal, Not Vertical Relationships
rawContent: When you praise or rebuke someone, you create a hierarchical (vertical) relationship, because praise involves a person of ability passing judgement on a person with no ability. For example, a parent praises a child for helping with dinner ('You're such a good helper!') but would thank an equal instead ('Thank you for helping'). Adlerian psychology is critical of reward-and-punishment in raising children because it manipulates them. If you derive joy from being praised, you are dependent on vertical relationships and living according to another person's values—not your own. This is not freedom. Vertical relationships give rise to inferiority complexes. In contrast, horizontal relationships are 'equal but not the same.' The most important thing is to avoid judging others. Instead of praise or rebuke, offer assistance and express gratitude and respect. A 'thank you' has no judgement. When someone hears gratitude, they know they've made a contribution. Once you build one vertical relationship, you will treat all relationships as vertical. Horizontal relationships are the key to freedom.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
If you get a rush from being praised, you're not free. You're living by someone else's values.

[BODY]
See, praise creates a hierarchy. When a parent tells a kid "You're such a good helper," they're passing judgement—person with ability to person without. But when you thank an equal, there's no judgement. Just "Thank you for helping." Now here's the thing: if you need praise to feel good, you're dependent on that vertical relationship. You're chasing someone else's approval instead of your own values. And once you build one vertical relationship, you start treating everyone that way. Adlerian psychology says this is how inferiority complexes start. The alternative? Horizontal relationships. Equal, but not the same. No judgement. Just gratitude and respect.

[CLOSE]
Next time you want to say "Good job," try "Thank you" instead. No hierarchy. Just contribution.

sourceRef: Build horizontal relationships and see others as comrades

title: Don't Get Drawn into Power Struggles
rawContent: If you feel angry due to another person's words or actions, they may be challenging you to a power struggle. Even if you 'win,' the other person will plot revenge and come back with retaliation. Once a relationship reaches the revenge stage, it's almost impossible to recover. When you feel challenged to a power struggle, you cannot be drawn into it. This doesn't mean 'grin and bear it'—that is still caving to the power struggle. Instead, communicate calmly without anger. It's okay to feel anger; just don't use it as a tool. Keep the discussion focused on the rightness of the arguments, not on who is right or wrong. If you start thinking 'I am right,' that implies the other person is wrong, setting up winners and losers. Once someone is in a mindset of not wanting to 'lose,' they won't admit mistakes, apologise, or step down. The key is to refuse the contest entirely and maintain a horizontal relationship where you're both equals seeking truth, not victory.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
When someone makes you angry, they're challenging you to a power struggle. And here's the thing: even if you win, you lose.

[BODY]
See, when you win a power struggle, the other person doesn't just accept defeat. They plot revenge. They come back with retaliation. And once a relationship hits that revenge stage, it's almost impossible to recover. So when someone challenges you, you can't take the bait. But this doesn't mean grin and bear it—that's still caving to the struggle. Instead, communicate calmly without anger. It's okay to feel angry. Just don't use it as a tool. Keep the discussion focused on what's right, not who's right. The moment you think "I am right," you're implying they're wrong. Now you've got winners and losers. And once someone's in a mindset of not wanting to lose, they won't admit mistakes. They won't apologize. They won't step down.

[CLOSE]
Refuse the contest entirely. Stay horizontal. You're both equals seeking truth, not victory.

sourceRef: Don't get drawn into power struggles

title: Community Feeling: The Goal of Relationships
rawContent: 'Community feeling' is the sense of seeing other people as comrades and having a sense of belonging. It is the goal of interpersonal relationships. You acquire community feeling through your own efforts by switching from self-interest to concern for others (social interest). To do this, you need three things: self-acceptance (accepting yourself as you are, not lying to yourself with affirmations), confidence in others (unconditional belief without conditions, unlike trust which has conditions), and contribution to others (helping others without self-sacrifice). These three link together: when you accept yourself, you can have confidence in others without fear; when you have confidence in others, you can contribute to them; and when you contribute, you gain awareness that you are of use and accept yourself. When you feel beneficial to the community—that you are of use to someone—you find happiness and a true sense of your own worth. That sense of worth gives you courage. Contribution to others is a 'guiding star' that helps when you lose your way.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
You're not going to feel like you belong until you stop focusing on yourself. See, Adlerian psychology has this concept called community feeling—it's the sense of seeing other people as comrades. And getting there requires three specific things.

[BODY]
First, self-acceptance. Not lying to yourself with affirmations like "I'm amazing." Just accepting yourself as you actually are right now. Second, confidence in others. This is different from trust. Trust has conditions—"I'll trust you if you do this." Confidence is unconditional belief. And third, contribution to others. Helping people without sacrificing yourself. Now here's the thing: these three link together. When you accept yourself, you can have confidence in others without fear of being hurt. When you have confidence in others, you can actually contribute to them. And when you contribute, you gain this awareness that you're useful—which makes you accept yourself even more. It's a cycle.

[CLOSE]
Next time you feel lost or worthless, ask yourself: who can I help today? That feeling of being useful to someone—that's where your sense of worth actually comes from.

sourceRef: The goal of interpersonal relationships is 'community feeling'

title: Happiness is the Feeling of Contribution
rawContent: Happiness is the feeling of contribution. By making contributions to and helping others, you feel you are beneficial to the community. Contribution to others does not mean self-sacrifice—Adler warns that those who sacrifice their lives for others have conformed to society too much. You can contribute simply by being. For example, a baby or an invalid contributes to their family's psychological state simply by being alive. You are not the one who decides if your contributions are useful—that is others' task. But there is no objective way to know if you've really made a contribution. All you have is the subjective sense that you are of use to someone—the feeling of contribution. It is only when you feel beneficial to the community that you find happiness and have a true sense of your own worth. That sense of worth gives you courage. As long as you keep moving in the direction of contribution to others, there is happiness.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Happiness isn't what you think. It's not pleasure. It's not comfort. It's the feeling of contribution.

[BODY]
See, when you help someone—even in a small way—you feel like you matter. Like you're useful to the people around you. Now, contribution doesn't mean sacrificing yourself. Adler warns against that. You don't have to burn yourself out for others. You can contribute just by being. A baby contributes to their family just by existing. An elderly parent contributes by being there. You're not the one who decides if your contributions are useful—that's their task, not yours. But here's the thing: there's no objective way to measure it. All you have is the subjective sense that you're of use to someone. That feeling—that's what gives you worth. And that worth gives you courage.

[CLOSE]
Keep moving in the direction of contribution. Even small acts. That's where happiness lives.

title: You Can Change: Lifestyle is a Choice
rawContent: Adlerian psychology says people first choose their lifestyle (personality, worldview, outlook on life) around age 10. It may not have been entirely conscious, but it was still a choice. And people can choose to continue with the lifestyle they've had or choose a new one entirely. The philosopher prefers talking about 'lifestyle' or 'worldview' rather than 'personality' because personality suggests it is unchangeable, whereas it's easier to accept that a worldview is alterable. The important thing is not what one is born with, but what use one makes of that equipment. If you change, your world will change. This means the world can be changed only by you, and no one else can change it for you. Our past does not determine our future. But change requires courage. Change generates anxiety, whereas not changing (when unhappy) generates disappointment. Adlerian psychology focuses on 'encouragement'—helping people get the courage to change.

script.claude-sonnet:
[HOOK]
Adlerian psychology says you chose your personality around age 10. Not consciously—but you still chose it. And here's the thing: you can choose a different one right now.

[BODY]
See, Adler doesn't call it personality. He calls it lifestyle. Because personality sounds permanent, like you're stuck with it. But lifestyle? That's something you can change. Now, changing requires courage. When you change, you feel anxious. When you stay the same and unhappy, you feel disappointed. Pick your pain. The past doesn't determine your future—what matters is what you do with what you've got. If you change, your world changes. No one else can do it for you.

[CLOSE]
So the real question is: do you have the courage to choose differently?

title: Internal vs External Triggers
rawContent: Internal triggers are psychological cues that prompt users to engage with a product without the need for an external stimulus. Unlike external triggers, which are sensory stimuli like alarms or buttons, internal triggers are thoughts or emotions that automatically manifest in the mind. These triggers can be powerful in forming habits, as they create a direct link between the user's needs and the product. For instance, a person might use a photo app like Instagram to satisfy the internal trigger of wanting to capture special moments, even without being prompted by an external cue. This psychological connection is the foundation of habit-forming technology.

script.default:
[HOOK]
Ever wonder why you reach for Instagram without even thinking about it?

[BODY]
It's because of something called internal triggers. These are psychological cues, like thoughts or emotions, that nudge you to use an app or product without any alarms or notifications. Take Instagram, for example. You might feel the urge to capture a special moment—that's an internal trigger at work. It's your mind automatically linking that feeling to the app. Over time, this connection becomes a habit. You don't need a ding or buzz; your own thoughts guide you back.

[CLOSE]
Next time you open an app, think about what internal trigger brought you there. It's the first step in understanding your habits.

title: source
rawContent:
Internal Triggers When a product becomes tightly coupled with a thought, an emotion, or a preexisting routine, it leverages an internal trigger. Unlike external triggers, which use sensory stimuli like a morning alarm clock or giant “Login Now” button, you can’t see, touch, or hear an internal trigger. Internal triggers manifest automatically in your mind. Connecting internal triggers with a product is the brass ring of habit-forming technology. For Yin, the young woman with the Instagram habit, her favorite photo app manufactured a predictable response cued by an internal trigger. Through repeated conditioning, a connection was formed between Yin’s need to capture images of the things around her and the app on her ever-present mobile device. Emotions, particularly negative ones, are powerful internal triggers and greatly influence our daily routines. Feelings of boredom, loneliness, frustration, confusion, and indecisiveness often instigate a slight pain or irritation and prompt an almost instantaneous and often mindless action to quell the negative sensation. For instance, Yin often uses Instagram when she fears a special moment will be lost forever. The severity of the discomfort may be relatively minor—perhaps her fear is below the perceptibility of consciousness—but that’s exactly the point. Our life is filled with tiny stressors and we’re usually unaware of our habitual reactions to these nagging issues. Positive emotions can also serve as internal triggers, and may even be triggered themselves by a need to satisfy something that is bothering us. After all, we use products to find solutions to problems. The desire to be entertained can be thought of as the need to satiate boredom. A need to share good news can also be thought of as an attempt to find and maintain social connections. As product designers it is our goal to solve these problems and eliminate pain—to scratch the user’s itch. Users who find a product that alleviates their pain will form strong, positive associations with the product over time. After continued use, bonds begin to form—like the layers of nacre in an oyster—between the product and the user whose need it satisfies. Gradually, these bonds cement into a habit as users turn to your product when experiencing certain internal triggers. A study at the Missouri University of Science and Technology illustrates how tech solutions can provide frequent psychological relief.6 In 2011 a group of 216 undergraduates volunteered to have their Internet activity anonymously tracked. Over the course of the academic year, the researchers measured the frequency with which these students used the web and what they were doing online. At the end of the study, the researchers compared anonymous data of students who visited the university’s health services to treat symptoms of depression. “We identified several features of Internet usage that correlated with depression,” wrote Sriram Chellappan, one of the study’s authors.7 “For example, participants with depressive symptoms tended to engage in very high e-mail usage . . . Other characteristic features of depressive Internet behavior included increased amounts of video watching, gaming, and chatting.” The study demonstrated that people suffering from symptoms of depression used the Internet more. Why is that? One hypothesis is that those with depression experience negative emotions more frequently than the general population and seek relief by turning to technology to lift their mood. Consider, perhaps, your own emotion-cued behaviors. What do you do in response to your internal triggers? When bored, many people seek excitement and turn to dramatic news headlines. When we feel overly stressed, we seek serenity, perhaps finding relief with a meditation app like Headspace. When we feel lonely, we might use a social network to provide connection. To ameliorate the sensation of uncertainty, Google is just a click away. E-mail, perhaps the mother of all habit-forming technology, is a go-to solution for many of our daily agitations, from validating our importance (or even our existence) by checking to see if someone needs us, to providing an escape from life’s more mundane moments. Once we’re hooked, using these products does not always require an explicit call to action. Instead, they rely upon our automatic responses to feelings that precipitate the desired behavior. Products that attach to these internal triggers provide users with quick relief. Once a technology has created an association in users’ minds that the product is the solution of choice, they return on their own, no longer needing prompts from external triggers. In the case of internal triggers, the information about what to do next is encoded as a learned association in the user’s memory. The association between an internal trigger and your product, however, is not formed overnight. It can take weeks or months of frequent usage for internal triggers to latch onto cues. New habits are sparked by external triggers, but associations with internal triggers are what keeps users hooked. As Yin said, “I just use it whenever I see something cool.” By thoughtfully moving users from external to internal triggers, Instagram designed a persistent routine in people’s lives. A need is triggered in Yin’s mind every time a moment is worth holding on to, and for her, the immediate solution is Instagram. Yin no longer requires an external stimulus to prompt her to use the app—the internal trigger happens on its own.

Eyal, Nir. Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products (pp. 47-51). Penguin Publishing Group. Kindle Edition.

[HOOK]
You're bored, so you open Instagram. You're lonely, so you scroll Twitter. You're stressed, so you check email. These aren't random—they're internal triggers. And once a product latches onto one, you're hooked.

[BODY]
An internal trigger is an emotion that cues a behavior. You can't see it or touch it—it just fires in your mind. Negative feelings like boredom or loneliness create a tiny pain, and your brain reaches for the fastest relief. A 2011 study at Missouri University tracked 216 students' internet use. Those with depression symptoms used email way more, watched more videos, gamed more, chatted more. Why? Because they felt bad more often—so they turned to tech for relief. Over time, the product becomes the automatic solution. You don't need a notification anymore. The feeling itself is the trigger.

script.default:
[PLANNING]
Hook: Stake the problem then introduce the concept
Why: Most people struggle with understanding why they automatically reach for their phones, and this sets up the explanation of internal triggers.

Body skeleton: Single concept
Body blocks: Define + Mechanism
Why: The content revolves around the concept of internal triggers, so defining it and explaining how it works is essential.

[HOOK]
Ever wonder why you grab your phone without even thinking when you're bored or stressed?

[BODY]
It's all about internal triggers. These are emotions or thoughts that cue you to take action—like reaching for Instagram when you're lonely or bored. Unlike an alarm buzzing in your ear, these triggers are invisible but powerful. They form through repeated use. For example, if you use Instagram whenever you see something cool, over time, your brain links that urge directly to the app. The app becomes your go-to relief, no external prompt needed.

[CLOSE]
Next time you find yourself reaching for your phone, pause and ask: what's triggering this? Identifying your internal triggers is the first step to understanding your habits.

title: source
rawContent:
Products that successfully create habits soothe the user’s pain by laying claim to a particular feeling. To do so, product designers must know their user’s internal triggers—that is, the pain they seek to solve. Finding customers’ internal triggers requires learning more about people than what they can tell you in a survey, though. It requires digging deeper to understand how your users feel. The ultimate goal of a habit-forming product is to solve the user’s pain by creating an association so that the user identifies the company’s product or service as the source of relief. First, the company must identify the particular frustration or pain point in emotional terms, rather than product features. How do you, as a designer, go about uncovering the source of a user’s pain? The best place to start is to learn the drivers behind successful habit-forming products—not to copy them, but to understand how they solve users’ problems. Doing so will give you practice in diving deeper into the mind of the consumer and alert you to common human needs and desires. As Evan Williams, cofounder of Blogger and Twitter said, the Internet is “a giant machine designed to give people what they want.”8 Williams continued, “We often think the Internet enables you to do new things . . . But people just want to do the same things they’ve always done.” These common needs are timeless and universal. Yet talking to users to reveal these wants will likely prove ineffective because they themselves don’t know which emotions motivate them. People just don’t think in these terms. You’ll often find that people’s declared preferences—what they say they want—are far different from their revealed preferences—what they actually do. As Erika Hall, author of Just Enough Research writes, “When the research focuses on what people actually do (watch cat videos) rather than what they wish they did (produce cinema-quality home movies) it actually expands possibilities.”9 Looking for discrepancies exposes opportunities. Why do people really send text messages? Why do they take photos? What role does watching television or sports play in their lives? Ask yourself what pain these habits solve and what the user might be feeling right before one of these actions. What would your users want to achieve by using your solution? Where and when will they use it? What emotions influence their use and will trigger them to action? Jack Dorsey, cofounder of Twitter and Square, shared how his companies answer these important questions: “[If] you want to build a product that is relevant to folks, you need to put yourself in their shoes and you need to write a story from their side. So, we spend a lot of time writing what’s called user narratives.”10 Dorsey goes on to describe how he tries to truly understand his user: “He is in the middle of Chicago and they go to a coffee store . . . This is the experience they’re going to have. It reads like a play. It’s really, really beautiful. If you do that story well, then all of the prioritization, all of the product, all of the design and all the coordination that you need to do with these products just falls out naturally because you can edit the story and everyone can relate to the story from all levels of the organization, engineers to operations to support to designers to the business side of the house.” Dorsey believes a clear description of users—their desires, emotions, the context with which they use the product—is paramount to building the right solution. In addition to Dorsey’s user narratives, tools like customer development,11 usability studies, and empathy maps12 are examples of methods for learning about potential users. One method is to try asking the question “Why?” as many times as it takes to get to an emotion. Usually, this will happen by the fifth why. This is a technique adapted from the Toyota Production System, described by Taiichi Ohno as the “5 Whys Method.” Ohno wrote that it was “the basis of Toyota’s scientific approach . . . by repeating ‘why?’ five times, the nature of the problem as well as its solution becomes clear.”13 When it comes to figuring out why people use habit-forming products, internal triggers are the root cause, and “Why?” is a question that can help drill right to the core. For example, let’s say we’re building a fancy new technology called e-mail for the first time. The target user is a busy middle manager named Julie. We’ve built a detailed narrative of our user, Julie, that helps us answer the following series of whys: Why #1: Why would Julie want to use e-mail? Answer: So she can send and receive messages. Why #2: Why would she want to do that? Answer: Because she wants to share and receive information quickly. Why #3: Why does she want to do that? Answer: To know what’s going on in the lives of her coworkers, friends, and family. Why #4: Why does she need to know that? Answer: To know if someone needs her. Why #5: Why would she care about that? Answer: She fears being out of the loop. Now we’ve got something! Fear is a powerful internal trigger and we can design our solution to help calm Julie’s fear. Naturally, we might have come to another conclusion by starting with a different persona, varying the narrative, or coming up with different hypothetical answers along the chain of whys. Only an accurate understanding of our user’s underlying needs can inform the product requirements. Now that we have an understanding of the user’s pain, we can move on to the next step of testing our product to see if it solves his problem. Unpacking Instagram’s Triggers A large component of Instagram’s success—and what brings its millions of users back nearly every day—is the company’s ability to understand its users’ triggers. For people like Yin, Instagram is a harbor for emotions and inspirations, a virtual memoir preserved in pixels. Yin’s habitual use of the service started with an external trigger—a recommendation from a friend and weeks of repetitious use before she became a regular user. Every time Yin snaps a picture, she shares it with her friends on Facebook. Consider the first time you saw an Instagram photo. Did it catch your attention? Did it make you curious? Did it call you to action? These photos serve as a relationship external trigger, raising awareness and serving as a cue for others to install and use the app. But Instagram photos shared on Facebook and Twitter were not the only external triggers driving new users. Others learned of the app from the media and bloggers, or through the featured placement Apple granted Instagram in its App Store—all earned external triggers. Once installed, Instagram benefited from owned external triggers. The app icon on users’ phone screens and push notifications about their friends’ postings served to call them back. With repeated use, Instagram formed strong associations with internal triggers, and what was once a brief behavior became an intraday routine for many users. It is the fear of losing a special moment that instigates a pang of stress. This negative emotion is the internal trigger that brings Instagram users back to the app to alleviate this pain by capturing a photo. Yet, as users continue to use the service, new internal triggers form. Instagram is more than a camera replacement; it is a social network. The app helps users dispel boredom by connecting them with others, sharing photos, and swapping lighthearted banter.14 Like many social networking sites, Instagram also alleviates the increasingly recognizable pain point known as fear of missing out, or FOMO. For Instagram, associations with internal triggers provide a foundation to form new habits. It is now time to understand the mechanics of connecting the user’s problem with your solution by utilizing the next step in the Hooked Model. In the next chapter we’ll find out how moving people from triggers to actions is critical in establishing new routines.

Eyal, Nir. Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products (pp. 51-56). Penguin Publishing Group. Kindle Edition.

script.mixed:
[PLANNING]
Hook: Stake the problem then introduce the concept
Why: Universal struggle—designers want users to return, but don't know what emotional pain to solve. Then introduce the "5 Whys" method as the tool to uncover it.

Body skeleton: List (3 steps)
Body blocks: Step 1 = Define+unpack (revealed vs declared preferences), Step 2 = Concrete example (Julie email story), Step 3 = Mechanism (how internal triggers form habits)
Why: Three discrete moves: understand the gap, ask why five times, design for the emotion. Each gets a different delivery mode.

[HOOK]
You can't build a habit-forming product if you don't know what pain you're solving. See, users don't know either—they can't tell you in a survey. The question is: how do you uncover the real emotion driving their behavior?

[BODY]
Step one: look for the gap between what people say they want and what they actually do. Erika Hall calls this declared versus revealed preferences. People say they want to make cinema-quality home movies. They actually watch cat videos. That gap is where the opportunity lives.

Step two: ask "Why?" five times. Toyota used this to find root causes. Let's say you're building email. Why would Julie use it? To send messages. Why? To share information quickly. Why? To know what's happening with coworkers and friends. Why? To know if someone needs her. Why does she care? She fears being out of the loop. Now you've got it—fear. That's the internal trigger.

Step three: design your product to soothe that emotion. Instagram doesn't just store photos—it calms the fear of losing a special moment. Boredom? It connects you with others. FOMO? It shows you what's happening right now.

[CLOSE]
Pick one user. Ask why five times. Find the emotion. Then build for that.
script.claude-sonnet:
[PLANNING]
Hook: Stake the problem then introduce the concept
Why: The struggle is universal—designers want to build sticky products but don't know what pain to solve. Then I introduce the "5 Whys" method as the tool that gets to emotional triggers.

Body skeleton: List (3 steps)
Body blocks: Step 1 = Define+unpack (revealed vs declared preferences), Step 2 = Concrete example (the email/Julie walkthrough), Step 3 = Mechanism (how the method exposes the root emotion)
Why: The content naturally breaks into: understanding the gap, applying the method, seeing the payoff. Each step builds on the last.

---

[HOOK]
You can't build a habit-forming product if you don't know what pain you're solving. See, users don't consciously know their own triggers. So here's the method: ask "Why?" five times.

[BODY]
Step one: understand that what people say they want isn't what they actually do. They'll tell you they want to make cinema-quality home movies. But they actually watch cat videos. That gap is where the opportunity lives. Step two: pick a user and ask why they'd use your product. Then ask why again. And again. Five times total. Let me show you. Say we're building email for the first time. User is Julie, a busy manager. Why would Julie use email? To send messages. Why? To share info quickly. Why? To know what's happening with coworkers and friends. Why does she need that? To know if someone needs her. Why does she care? She fears being out of the loop. Now we've got it—fear. Step three: design your solution to calm that fear. The fifth why gets you to the emotion. That's the internal trigger you're solving for.

[CLOSE]
Pick one feature you're building. Ask why five times. Find the fear, boredom, or loneliness underneath.
script.default:
[PLANNING]  
Hook: Stake the problem then introduce the concept  
Why: Many people struggle to understand what truly drives user behavior. This approach highlights the gap and introduces a process to uncover deep motivations.

Body skeleton: Single concept  
Body blocks: Define + unpack, Concrete example  
Why: The single concept of internal triggers is key. We'll define it and show how the “5 Whys” method digs deeper into user motivations, using a relatable example for clarity.

[HOOK]  
Ever wonder why some products become habits while others don't? It all starts with understanding internal triggers.

[BODY]  
Internal triggers are the emotions or pain points that drive someone to use a product. But here's the catch: users often can't articulate these triggers. So how do you find them? One effective method is the "5 Whys." You keep asking "why" until you get to the root emotion. Take email, for example. Why do people use it? To send messages. Why send messages? To stay informed. Keep digging, and you might find it’s driven by a fear of being out of the loop. That fear is the real trigger.

[CLOSE]  
Next time you're designing, remember: dig deep to uncover what truly motivates your users. The "5 Whys" can reveal the emotions that make your product indispensable.
